walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Pandemic set up. First, unfold the board and place it in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. The board shows a world map with cities connected by white lines, plus several tracks along the edges. Take a moment to notice the Outbreaks Track on the bottom left, the Infection Rate Track at the top, and the four Cure Indicators at the bottom.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Disease Cubes
walkthrough.text: Now find the ninety-six disease cubes. They come in four colors: blue, yellow, black, and red. Separate them into four piles by color and place them near the board where everyone can reach them. These cubes represent the diseases spreading across the world, and we will be placing and removing them throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Place the Research Stations
walkthrough.text: Find the six research stations. These are small plastic pieces that look like little buildings. Set five of them aside near the board as a supply. Take the sixth one and place it on the city of Atlanta. Atlanta is our home base because it is where the CDC is located in real life.

walkthrough.title: Set the Outbreaks Marker
walkthrough.text: Find the outbreaks marker. Place it on the zero space of the Outbreaks Track on the board. This tracks how many outbreaks have happened. If it ever reaches eight, we lose the game immediately, so we will want to keep a close eye on it.

walkthrough.title: Place the Cure Markers
walkthrough.text: Find the four cure markers. Each one has two sides: a vial side and a sunset-burst eradicated side. Place all four of them vial-side up near the Discovered Cure Indicators at the bottom of the board. When we discover a cure for a disease, we will move its marker onto the matching indicator.

walkthrough.title: Set the Infection Rate
walkthrough.text: Find the infection rate marker and place it on the leftmost two space of the Infection Rate Track at the top of the board. This number tells us how many Infection cards we flip at the end of each turn. It starts at two and increases as Epidemic cards are drawn.

walkthrough.title: Seed the Infections
walkthrough.text: Now let's infect the world. Shuffle the Infection deck and draw three cards. Place three disease cubes of each card's matching color on each of those three cities. Draw three more cards and place two cubes on each of those cities. Draw three more and place one cube on each. That is nine cards and eighteen cubes total. Place all nine drawn cards face up in the Infection Discard Pile.

walkthrough.title: Form the Infection Deck
walkthrough.text: The remaining Infection cards stay face down as the Infection Deck. Place it near the Infection Discard Pile on the board. Take a moment to look at the board and notice which cities already have cubes. The cities with three cubes are our most dangerous hot zones right from the start.

walkthrough.title: Deal Roles
walkthrough.text: Give each player a reference card. Then shuffle the seven role cards and deal one face up to each player. Each role has a unique special ability that changes how you play. Find the matching colored pawn for each dealt role and place all pawns in Atlanta. Put unused role cards and pawns back in the box.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Before dealing hands, find all six Epidemic cards and set them aside. Shuffle the remaining Player cards, which include City cards and Event cards. Deal cards based on player count: four cards each for two players, three cards each for three players, or two cards each for four players.

walkthrough.title: Choose Difficulty
walkthrough.text: Now let's set our difficulty. For a first game, use four Epidemic cards. That is the Introductory level. Five Epidemic cards is the Standard game, and six is Heroic. Put any unused Epidemic cards back in the box. We recommend starting with four until everyone feels comfortable with the flow of the game.

walkthrough.title: Build the Player Deck
walkthrough.text: Divide the remaining Player cards into face-down piles equal to the number of Epidemic cards you are using. Shuffle one Epidemic card into each pile. Then stack the piles together to form the Player Deck, placing smaller piles on the bottom. This ensures Epidemics are spread throughout the game rather than clumped together.

walkthrough.title: Pick the First Player
walkthrough.text: Everyone look at the City cards in your hand. The player holding the city with the highest population goes first. Population numbers are printed right on each City card. Play will proceed clockwise from the first player.

walkthrough.title: The Three-Part Turn
walkthrough.text: Every turn in Pandemic has three parts, always in the same order. First, the active player takes up to four actions. Second, they draw two Player cards from the Player Deck. Third, they infect cities by drawing Infection cards. After all three parts, the player on the left takes their turn. Let's walk through each part.

walkthrough.title: Actions Overview
walkthrough.text: On our turn, we get four actions. We can mix and match any combination from eight possible actions, and we can repeat the same action multiple times. Each repetition counts as one of our four actions. The eight actions fall into three groups: movement actions, board actions, and card actions.

walkthrough.title: Drive or Ferry
walkthrough.text: The simplest movement is Drive or Ferry. We move our pawn to a city connected to our current city by a white line on the board. Each move along one line costs one action. Some white lines wrap around the edges of the map, so cities like Sydney and Los Angeles are actually connected.

walkthrough.title: Direct Flight
walkthrough.text: For Direct Flight, we discard a City card from our hand to fly directly to the city shown on that card. It does not matter where we are on the board. This is great for crossing the map quickly, but remember that every City card we discard is one fewer card toward discovering a cure.

walkthrough.title: Charter Flight
walkthrough.text: Charter Flight is the reverse of Direct Flight. We discard the City card that matches the city we are currently in, and then we can fly to any city on the board. This is the most flexible movement option, but it requires us to be holding our current city's card.

walkthrough.title: Shuttle Flight
walkthrough.text: If we are in a city with a research station, we can use Shuttle Flight to move to any other city that also has a research station. This costs one action and does not require discarding any cards. Building research stations in key locations creates a free travel network for the whole team.

walkthrough.title: Build a Research Station
walkthrough.text: We can discard the City card matching our current city to build a research station there. Research stations serve two big purposes: they enable Shuttle Flights for fast travel, and they are required to Discover a Cure. If all six stations are already on the board, we can move one from another city to our current city.

walkthrough.title: Treat Disease
walkthrough.text: The Treat Disease action lets us remove one disease cube from the city we are in. If that color disease has already been cured, one Treat action removes all cubes of that color from our city instead. If we remove the last cube of a cured disease from the entire board, that disease is eradicated and no new cubes of that color will ever be placed.

walkthrough.title: Share Knowledge
walkthrough.text: To Share Knowledge, we either give or take the City card that matches the city both players are standing in. Both players must be in the same city and both must agree to the trade. This is the primary way to collect sets of same-color cards for curing diseases. The receiving player must immediately discard down to seven cards if they go over the hand limit.

walkthrough.title: Discover a Cure
walkthrough.text: This is how we win the game. At any research station, we can discard five City cards of the same color to cure that disease. Move the matching cure marker to the Cure Indicator. We do not need to be at a station matching the disease color. Curing a disease does not remove cubes from the board, but it makes treating that disease much easier.

walkthrough.title: Drawing Player Cards
walkthrough.text: After taking our four actions, we draw two cards from the Player Deck. If we draw a City card or Event card, it goes into our hand. If there are fewer than two cards left in the Player Deck when we need to draw, the game ends and we lose immediately. There is no reshuffling the discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Epidemic Cards
walkthrough.text: If we draw an Epidemic card, we resolve it immediately in three steps. First, Increase: move the infection rate marker forward one space. Second, Infect: draw the bottom card of the Infection Deck and place three cubes of that color on the named city. Third, Intensify: shuffle the entire Infection Discard Pile and place it on top of the Infection Deck. Then remove the Epidemic card from the game.

walkthrough.title: Why Intensify Is Dangerous
walkthrough.text: The Intensify step is what makes Pandemic so tense. All the cities that were recently infected get shuffled back on top of the Infection Deck, which means they will be drawn again very soon. Cities that already have two or three cubes are now at high risk of outbreaks. This is the core pressure mechanism of the game.

walkthrough.title: Hand Limit
walkthrough.text: After resolving any Epidemic cards from our draw, we check our hand size. The hand limit is seven cards. If we have more than seven, we must immediately discard cards or play Event cards until we are back down to seven. Plan ahead so we do not have to throw away cards we need for cures.

walkthrough.title: Infect Cities
walkthrough.text: The final step of our turn is infecting cities. We flip Infection cards from the top of the Infection Deck, one at a time, equal to the current infection rate. For each card, we place one cube of the matching color on that city. If the disease has been eradicated, we skip it. Discard each Infection card to the Infection Discard Pile after resolving it.

walkthrough.title: Outbreaks
walkthrough.text: If we ever need to place a disease cube on a city that already has three cubes of that color, an outbreak occurs. We move the outbreaks marker forward one space and place one cube of that color on every city connected to the outbreak city. If any of those connected cities also overflow past three cubes, chain reaction outbreaks happen. A city can only outbreak once per chain, but multiple cities can chain together in a single turn.

walkthrough.title: Event Cards
walkthrough.text: Event cards can be played at any time, even on another player's turn. Playing an Event card is not an action, so it does not use up one of our four actions. Each Event card has unique instructions printed on it. After playing an Event, discard it to the Player Discard Pile. The only restriction is that we cannot play Events in between drawing and resolving a card.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing
walkthrough.text: We win immediately when we discover cures for all four diseases. We do not need to eradicate them or remove all cubes from the board. We lose if any of three things happen: the outbreaks marker reaches eight, we cannot place a cube because that color's supply is empty, or a player cannot draw two Player cards because the deck is exhausted. Keep all three loss conditions in mind at all times.

summary.title: The Three-Part Turn
bullets: 1. Take up to 4 actions
2. Draw 2 Player cards (resolve Epidemics immediately)
3. Infect cities (draw Infection cards = current infection rate)

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Pandemic
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Pandemic. In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first few turns of a four-player game. We will see each part of a turn in action, watch some common beginner mistakes unfold, and learn how to think cooperatively as a team. Follow along even if you have fewer players at your table.

walkthrough.title: Meet the Team
walkthrough.text: Let's say our four players have drawn these roles: the Medic, the Scientist, the Researcher, and the Operations Expert. All four pawns start in Atlanta, where a research station is already built. The board has been seeded with eighteen cubes across nine cities. Take a look at which cities have three cubes. Those are our first priority.

walkthrough.title: Assess the Board
walkthrough.text: Before anyone takes a turn, let's talk as a team. Look at the three-cube cities. If any of them get infected again, they will outbreak immediately. Check if any of those cities are near each other. Two three-cube cities connected by a white line could trigger a chain reaction outbreak, which is catastrophic this early. Our first moves should address the most dangerous clusters.

walkthrough.title: Turn 1: The Medic Moves Out
walkthrough.text: The Medic goes first because they had the highest population card. The Medic is our best firefighter because their Treat Disease action removes all cubes of one color from a city. Let's send the Medic toward the nearest three-cube city. If it is two cities away, the Medic can Drive twice and then Treat Disease twice, clearing cubes from two danger zones in one turn.

walkthrough.title: The Medic Draws Cards
walkthrough.text: After the Medic's four actions, they draw two Player cards. Let's say they draw a blue City card and a yellow City card. No Epidemics yet, which is a relief. The Medic holds these cards and then moves to the infection step. The Medic flips two Infection cards and places one cube on each named city. Our turn is done.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Spreading Too Thin
walkthrough.text: The Scientist is up next. The Scientist looks at the board and sees cubes in many different places. They decide to fly across the map to treat a single one-cube city on the other side of the world, burning a City card for Direct Flight. This is a classic beginner mistake. That one-cube city is not dangerous. Meanwhile, the Scientist just wasted an action and a valuable City card that could have contributed to a cure.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Prioritize Three-Cube Cities
walkthrough.text: Here is the lesson: one-cube cities are not emergencies. Three-cube cities are. If a three-cube city gets infected again, it outbreaks and can cascade into neighboring cities. Always prioritize treating cities with three cubes, especially if they are connected to other high-cube cities. Save your movement cards for when they truly matter.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: The Researcher Plans Ahead
walkthrough.text: The Researcher has a special ability: when Sharing Knowledge, they can give any City card from their hand, not just the one matching their current city. This makes the Researcher the team's best card courier. Let's have the Researcher look at what colors the Scientist needs and start moving toward a meeting point. The Researcher drives two cities toward the Scientist and treats a two-cube city along the way.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring Share Knowledge
walkthrough.text: The Operations Expert takes their turn and has three black City cards. They spend all four actions driving around treating one-cube cities, completely ignoring the fact that the Scientist has two black cards and only needs four total to cure black. If the Operations Expert had moved toward the Scientist instead, they could Share Knowledge on a future turn and set up a cure. Treating minor infections feels productive but delays the real goal.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Curing Wins the Game
walkthrough.text: Treating disease keeps us alive, but curing disease wins the game. Every turn we spend cleaning up one-cube cities is a turn we are not assembling the five cards needed for a cure. The best teams balance containment with cure progress. Always ask: is this action bringing us closer to curing a disease, or just treading water?

walkthrough.title: First Epidemic Strikes
walkthrough.text: On the Medic's second turn, they draw an Epidemic card. Let's resolve it together. First, move the infection rate marker forward one space. Second, draw the bottom card of the Infection Deck and place three cubes on that city. Third, shuffle the entire Infection Discard Pile and place it on top of the Infection Deck. Now all those previously infected cities are coming back around soon.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring Outbreak Chains
walkthrough.text: After the Epidemic, the infection step hits a city that already has three cubes. An outbreak occurs. The team panics about the outbreak city but forgets to check the connected cities. One of the connected cities also now has three cubes from the outbreak, and on the very next turn it gets infected too, causing a chain reaction. Two outbreaks in quick succession because nobody treated the neighbors.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch the Neighbors
walkthrough.text: When a city hits three cubes, immediately check all cities connected to it. If any of those neighbors also have two or three cubes, they are chain reaction risks. After an Epidemic, look at the Infection Discard Pile before it gets shuffled. Those are the cities coming back to the top of the deck. If any of them have three cubes, we need someone there to treat before the next infection step.

walkthrough.title: Building a Research Station
walkthrough.text: The Operations Expert uses their special ability to build a research station without discarding a card. They place it in a central hub city on the board. Now the team can Shuttle Flight between Atlanta and this new station for free. Strategic station placement cuts travel time dramatically. Think of research stations as our fast travel network.

walkthrough.title: Setting Up a Cure
walkthrough.text: The Researcher and the Scientist meet in the same city. The Researcher gives the Scientist two black City cards using Share Knowledge. Combined with the cards the Scientist already has, they now hold four black cards. Since the Scientist only needs four cards to Discover a Cure, they can cure black disease on their next turn if they can reach a research station.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Wrong Station Position
walkthrough.text: The Scientist has four black cards and wants to cure, but the nearest research station is three cities away. That is three Drive actions just to get there, leaving only one action to actually Discover a Cure. If the team had planned ahead and built a station closer to the Scientist's path, or if the Scientist had used a Direct Flight, they could have cured a turn earlier. Every turn matters when the deck is ticking down.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Station Placement
walkthrough.text: Research stations should be placed strategically, not just wherever someone happens to be standing. Think about where card handoffs will happen and where cure attempts will take place. Central cities with many connections make the best station locations. Two or three well-placed stations can dramatically reduce the team's travel overhead.

walkthrough.title: The First Cure
walkthrough.text: The Scientist reaches a research station and discards four black City cards to Discover a Cure. We move the black cure marker to the Black Cure Indicator. Congratulations, that is one of four cures done. Now when anyone uses Treat Disease on a black city, they remove all black cubes with a single action instead of just one. This makes the Medic even more powerful against black disease.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Hoarding Event Cards
walkthrough.text: One player has been holding an Event card for the entire game, waiting for the perfect moment to play it. Meanwhile, an outbreak happens that the Event card could have prevented. Event cards are free to play and do not cost an action. Do not save them for a hypothetical perfect scenario. If an Event card can prevent an outbreak or buy the team a crucial turn, play it now.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues around the table. Each turn follows the same rhythm: four actions, draw two cards, infect cities. The team should constantly be communicating about who has which cards, where the biggest threats are, and which cure to pursue next. Remember, this is a cooperative game. Talk openly about your hand, your plan, and what help you need.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations, you have walked through the core flow of Pandemic. You have seen how turns work, watched outbreaks cascade, set up a cure through Share Knowledge, and learned from five common beginner mistakes: spreading too thin, ignoring card sharing, missing outbreak chains, poor station placement, and hoarding Event cards. You are ready to play a full game on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your first real game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and key decisions every team faces.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all seven roles, actions, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Good luck saving the world!

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for new teams
Reference the Appendix → quick guide to roles, actions, and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the biggest traps new teams fall into. First, treating one-cube cities while three-cube cities are about to outbreak. Second, discarding City cards for flights when those cards are needed for cures. Third, playing solo instead of coordinating card trades with teammates. Fourth, building research stations randomly instead of in strategic hub locations. Fifth, hoarding Event cards until the game is already lost. Keep these in mind and your team will survive much longer.

walkthrough.title: The Cure Race
walkthrough.text: Pandemic is a race against the clock. Every turn, the Player Deck gets smaller, and when it runs out, we lose. That means we cannot waste turns. Every action should either prevent an immediate disaster or bring us closer to curing a disease. A good rule of thumb is to aim for our first cure by turns four through six, and to have a clear plan for the second cure before the first one is done. If we reach the midpoint of the game with zero cures, we are in serious trouble.

walkthrough.title: Card Management
walkthrough.text: City cards are the most important resource in the game. We need five of the same color to cure a disease, and we only draw two per turn. That means every card we discard for a flight or a research station is a card we cannot use for a cure. Before discarding any card, ask the team: does anyone need this color? The Researcher's ability to trade any card makes them a critical piece of the card pipeline. Route cards through the Researcher whenever possible.

walkthrough.title: Role Synergies
walkthrough.text: Each role has a strength, and the best teams play to those strengths. The Medic clears disease most efficiently, so send them to hot zones. The Scientist cures with four cards instead of five, so funnel cards to them first. The Researcher can give any card regardless of location match, so they are the team's card courier. The Operations Expert builds stations for free, creating the fast travel network. The Dispatcher can move other players' pawns, setting up card trades. Match roles to jobs and the team runs like a machine.

walkthrough.title: Containment vs Cure Push
walkthrough.text: Every turn presents a choice: do we contain the board, or do we push for a cure? Containment means treating diseases and preventing outbreaks. Cure push means moving toward card trades and cure attempts. The answer depends on the board state. If multiple cities have three cubes and an Epidemic is due soon, containment takes priority. If the board is stable and someone is close to five same-color cards, push for the cure. Getting a cure done early makes containment easier because Treat Disease becomes stronger for that color.

walkthrough.title: Station Placement Strategy
walkthrough.text: Research stations serve two purposes: enabling Shuttle Flights and providing locations to Discover a Cure. Place stations in central cities with many connections, not in corner cities. Good station cities include those that bridge different regions of the map. Two or three well-placed stations can cut average travel between any two points from four actions down to two. Plan station placement as a team, not as an afterthought.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Infection Deck
walkthrough.text: After an Epidemic, the Infection Discard Pile gets shuffled and placed on top of the Infection Deck. This means we know exactly which cities are coming back soon, just not the order. Before the shuffle, look at the discard pile together. If any of those cities currently have two or three cubes, someone needs to treat them before they come up again. This predictability is our biggest strategic advantage against the disease spread.

walkthrough.title: Team Communication
walkthrough.text: Pandemic is a cooperative game, and communication is our strongest tool. At the start of each round of turns, take a moment to plan together. Share what cards you have, discuss who is closest to a cure, and agree on priorities. The active player makes final decisions, but everyone should contribute ideas. Avoid the quarterbacking trap where one player makes all the decisions. Let everyone think and participate. The best wins come from genuine teamwork.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't treat 1-cube cities while 3-cube cities threaten outbreaks
Don't discard cure-color cards for flights unless absolutely necessary
Don't play solo — coordinate card trades with teammates
Don't build stations randomly — place them in strategic hubs
Don't hoard Event cards — play them when they prevent disasters

summary.title: The Cure Race
bullets: Player Deck is the clock — every turn draws closer to loss
Aim for first cure by turns 4-6
Plan second cure before finishing the first
Midgame with 0 cures = serious trouble

summary.title: Card Management
bullets: 5 same-color City cards = 1 cure; only draw 2 cards/turn
Before discarding for flights, ask: does anyone need this color?
Route cards through the Researcher for efficient trades
Plan hand limit around upcoming Share Knowledge opportunities

summary.title: Role Synergies
bullets: Medic → hot zones, best firefighter
Scientist → priority cure target, needs only 4 cards
Researcher → card courier, gives any card from hand
Operations Expert → free stations, travel network builder
Dispatcher → moves other pawns, sets up card trades

summary.title: Containment vs Cure Push
bullets: Board has multiple 3-cube cities → contain first
Board is stable + someone close to 5 cards → push for cure
Each cure makes containment easier (Treat removes all cubes)
Early cures create a positive feedback loop

summary.title: Station Placement Strategy
bullets: Place in central cities with many connections
Bridges between regions = best station locations
2-3 stations cut travel from ~4 actions to ~2
Plan placement as a team, not ad hoc

summary.title: Reading the Infection Deck
bullets: After Epidemic, discard pile goes on top → those cities return soon
Check discard pile before Intensify shuffle
2-3 cube cities in the discard = treat immediately
Predictability is your biggest strategic advantage

summary.title: Team Communication
bullets: Plan together at the start of each round of turns
Share card info openly, agree on priorities
Active player decides, but everyone contributes
Avoid quarterbacking — let all players participate

walkthrough.title: Card Funneling
walkthrough.text: The most efficient path to a cure is funneling cards to one player. Identify which player has the most cards of a single color, or which player has a role advantage like the Scientist. Then route matching cards to them through Share Knowledge chains. The Researcher is the ultimate funnel tool because they can hand over any card. Plan card trades two or three turns ahead so players are in position to share at the right time.

walkthrough.title: Epidemic Timing
walkthrough.text: Since we built the Player Deck by shuffling one Epidemic into each equal pile, we can roughly estimate when the next Epidemic will hit. Count how many Player cards have been drawn and compare to the deck size. If we are due for an Epidemic soon, pre-position the Medic or Quarantine Specialist near three-cube cities. After the Intensify step, we know which cities are on top of the Infection Deck. Treat those cities before they come up.

walkthrough.title: Triage and Sacrifice
walkthrough.text: Sometimes we cannot save everything. Advanced teams learn to triage: letting a one-cube or two-cube city outbreak is acceptable if it prevents a much worse chain reaction elsewhere. An outbreak only costs one space on the outbreak track. A chain reaction costs two or three. Focus containment on cities whose outbreaks would cascade. Let isolated outbreaks happen if it means we can save actions for curing.

walkthrough.title: Eradication Value
walkthrough.text: Eradication is not required to win, but it is extremely powerful when achievable. Once a disease is eradicated, its cubes are never placed again during infections or Epidemics. If we cure a color early and most of its cubes are already off the board, spending a few actions to remove the last cubes can save many actions in the long run. The Medic is the best eradicator because one Treat action clears an entire city of a cured color.

walkthrough.title: Dispatcher Tactics
walkthrough.text: The Dispatcher is one of the most powerful roles in the game when used aggressively. They can move any player's pawn as if it were their own, which means they can set up card trades that would otherwise take multiple turns to arrange. A classic combo: the Dispatcher moves the Researcher to the Scientist's city, and the Researcher immediately gives cure-color cards when their turn comes. The Dispatcher can also use their own City cards for Direct or Charter Flights when moving other players' pawns.

walkthrough.title: Quarantine Specialist Positioning
walkthrough.text: The Quarantine Specialist prevents cube placement in their city and all connected cities. This makes them incredibly powerful when parked in a central city with many connections, essentially creating a disease-free zone. After an Epidemic, check which cities are coming back to the top of the Infection Deck. If the Quarantine Specialist is adjacent to any of them, those cities are protected. Position the Quarantine Specialist proactively based on predicted infections rather than reactively after outbreaks.

walkthrough.title: Operations Expert Mobility
walkthrough.text: The Operations Expert has two powerful abilities that synergize together. They can build research stations without discarding a City card, and once per turn they can discard any City card to move from a research station to any city. This makes them the most mobile player on the board. Use them to build a network of stations early, then leverage those stations for Shuttle Flights. The Operations Expert can also carry cure cards across the map quickly by using their special movement to reach a card trade meeting point.

walkthrough.title: Late Game Endgame Push
walkthrough.text: When the Player Deck is running low and we still need one or two cures, it is time for an all-out endgame push. Stop treating disease unless an outbreak would end the game. Focus every action on getting cards to the right player and getting that player to a research station. Use Event cards aggressively to buy time. If we have the Airlift Event, use it to teleport the cure holder to a station. If we have One Quiet Night, play it to skip an infection phase and buy one more turn. The last few turns should feel like a sprint, not a marathon.

summary.title: Card Funneling
bullets: Identify the player closest to 5 same-color cards
Route matching cards through Share Knowledge chains
Researcher = ultimate funnel tool (gives any card)
Plan trades 2-3 turns ahead for positioning

summary.title: Epidemic Timing
bullets: Estimate next Epidemic by counting drawn Player cards vs deck size
Pre-position Medic/Quarantine Specialist near 3-cube cities
After Intensify, treat cities that shuffled back on top
Count Epidemics drawn vs total to gauge remaining risk

summary.title: Triage and Sacrifice
bullets: Isolated outbreak = 1 space on track, acceptable trade-off
Chain reaction = 2-3 spaces, must prevent
Focus containment on cascade-prone clusters
Letting minor outbreaks happen frees actions for cures

summary.title: Eradication Value
bullets: Not required to win, but removes a disease permanently
Worthwhile if most cubes already off board after curing
Medic = best eradicator (clears all cubes of cured color in 1 action)
Saves many future actions by eliminating a disease entirely

summary.title: Dispatcher Tactics
bullets: Move any pawn as if your own (with player permission)
Classic combo: move Researcher to Scientist for instant card trade
Uses Dispatcher's own cards for Direct/Charter Flight of other pawns
One of the most powerful roles when used proactively

summary.title: Quarantine Specialist Positioning
bullets: Prevents cube placement in current city AND all connected cities
Park in central city = disease-free zone covering many cities
Position based on predicted infections, not reactive cleanup
Strongest after Epidemic when known cities return to deck top

summary.title: Operations Expert Mobility
bullets: Builds stations without discarding a City card
Once per turn: discard any card to move from station to any city
Most mobile role — builds network + uses it for fast travel
Carry cure cards across map via special movement

summary.title: Late Game Endgame Push
bullets: Stop treating unless outbreak would end the game
Focus all actions on card trades + getting cure holder to station
Use Events aggressively: Airlift to station, One Quiet Night for tempo
Last turns = sprint for remaining cures

entries.definition: Role. Removes all cubes of one color when using Treat Disease (instead of just one). If a disease is cured, the Medic automatically removes all cubes of that color simply by entering or being in a city, without using an action. Also prevents cured-disease cubes from being placed in the Medic's location.

Role. Needs only four City cards of the same color (instead of five) to Discover a Cure. This makes the Scientist the highest-priority target for card funneling, since the team needs to collect one fewer card.

Role. When using Share Knowledge, the Researcher may give any City card from their hand to another player in the same city, without it having to match the current city. The transfer can happen on either player's turn. This makes the Researcher the team's best card courier.

Role. May build a research station in their current city without discarding a City card. Once per turn, may discard any City card to move from a research station to any city on the board. These abilities make the Operations Expert the most mobile player and the primary station builder.

Role. May move another player's pawn as if it were their own (with the player's permission), using the Dispatcher's own cards for Direct or Charter Flights. May also move any pawn to a city containing another pawn as an action. Cannot direct other players to perform actions like Treat Disease.

Role. Prevents disease cube placements and outbreaks in the city they are in and all cities connected to it. This protection does not affect cubes placed during initial setup. Most effective when positioned in a central, well-connected city.

Role. May take a discarded Event card from the Player Discard Pile and store it on their role card as an action. Only one Event card may be stored at a time, and it does not count toward the hand limit. When the stored Event is played, it is removed from the game instead of discarded.

Movement action. Move your pawn to a city connected to your current city by a white line on the board. Costs one action per move. Some lines wrap around the board edges, connecting cities on opposite sides of the map.

Movement action. Discard a City card from your hand to move your pawn to the city named on that card. Costs one action plus one City card. Useful for crossing the map quickly, but consumes a card that might be needed for a cure.
Movement action. Discard the City card that matches your current city to move to any city on the board. The most flexible movement option, but requires holding the card of your current location.

Movement action. Move from a city with a research station to any other city that also has a research station. No card discard required. Costs one action. The primary incentive for building multiple research stations.

Action. Discard the City card matching your current city to place a research station there. If all six stations are already built, relocate one from another city. Research stations enable Shuttle Flights and are required to Discover a Cure.

Action. Remove one disease cube from your current city. If that disease color has been cured, remove all cubes of that color instead. If the last cube of a cured disease is removed from the board, the disease is eradicated.

Action. Give or take the City card matching your current city to or from another player in the same city. Both players must agree. The receiving player must immediately discard to the seven-card hand limit if exceeded. The Researcher may give any card, not just the matching one.

Action. At any research station, discard five City cards of the same color to cure that disease. The Scientist needs only four. Move the cure marker to the Cure Indicator. Curing does not remove cubes from the board but makes Treat Disease more powerful for that color.

Triggered when a fourth cube of the same color would be placed on a city. Move the outbreaks marker forward one space and place one cube of that color on every connected city. Can cause chain reactions if connected cities also overflow. Each city can only outbreak once per infection card resolution.

When an outbreak places a cube on a connected city that already has three cubes of that color, that city also outbreaks. The outbreaks marker advances again. The original outbreak city does not receive cubes again during this chain. Multiple cities can chain together in a single resolution.

A special Player Deck card resolved in three steps: Increase (infection rate marker advances one space), Infect (draw bottom Infection card and place three cubes on that city), Intensify (shuffle Infection Discard Pile onto top of Infection Deck). The Epidemic card is then removed from the game.

The number of Infection cards drawn at the end of each turn. Starts at two and increases as Epidemic cards are drawn. The infection rate marker moves along the Infection Rate Track on the board. Higher rates mean more cities are infected per turn.

Occurs when the last cube of a cured disease is removed from the board. Flip the cure marker to its eradicated side. An eradicated disease never places new cubes during infections or Epidemics. Eradication is not required to win but is very powerful.

A structure placed on a city that enables Shuttle Flights between stations and is required to Discover a Cure. Maximum of six on the board at once. One begins in Atlanta during setup. The Operations Expert can build them without discarding a card.

The main draw deck containing City cards, Event cards, and Epidemic cards. Players draw two cards per turn after their actions. When the deck runs out and a player cannot draw, the team loses. The deck functions as the game's timer.

A separate deck of cards, one for each city on the board. Cards are drawn from the top during the infection step. After an Epidemic's Intensify step, the Infection Discard Pile is shuffled and placed on top of this deck, causing recently infected cities to appear again.

Players may hold a maximum of seven cards in hand. If a player exceeds seven cards (after drawing or receiving via Share Knowledge), they must immediately discard or play Event cards until at seven. Role cards and reference cards do not count toward the limit.

A double-sided token representing a disease's status. Vial side up means uncured. When a cure is discovered, the marker moves to the Cure Indicator. When the disease is eradicated, the marker is flipped to its eradicated side.

Special Player Deck cards that can be played at any time, even on another player's turn. Playing an Event is not an action. Events include Airlift, One Quiet Night, Government Grant, Resilient Population, and Forecast. After use, the card is discarded to the Player Discard Pile.

Event card. Move any one pawn to any city. Requires permission before moving another player's pawn. Powerful for teleporting a player to a research station for a cure attempt or rushing someone to a hot zone.
Event card. Skip the next Infect Cities step entirely. No Infection cards are drawn. Useful for buying time when multiple three-cube cities are at risk of outbreak.

Event card. Build a research station in any city without discarding a City card. Does not require a player to be in that city. Useful for placing stations in remote locations or saving a critical City card.

Event card. Remove any one card from the Infection Discard Pile from the game. Can be played between the Infect and Intensify steps of an Epidemic. Best used on a city with three cubes that is about to be shuffled back onto the deck.

Event card. Look at the top six cards of the Infection Deck and rearrange them in any order. Allows the team to control which cities are infected next, pushing dangerous cities to the bottom and safe cities to the top.
